text: Før jul hadde vi vår første episode om kjernekraft.
Nå har vi laget en oppfølger.
Dene gangen har jeg med meg Chris Kudomsky, som leder kjernekraftanalyset hos Bloomberg New Energy Finance.
Han er basert på vestkysten i USA, men kjenner kjernekraft globalt bedre enn de fleste vi våg å påstå.
Selv synes jeg Chris gir et både troverde og balansert bilde av hvor kjernekraft er på vei.
Og det tror jeg er nytt å ta med seg som investor.
Enten det er kjernekraftindeksfondet til D&B, enkeltakser eller andre måter man vurderer å ta eksponering på.
Så med det snurrer vi gang, og da jen står det bare å si, tusen takk for at du hører på utbytte.
Så jeg er med på kjernet med Chris Kudomsky, leder kjernekraftanalyset hos Bloomberg New Energy Finance. Chris, velkommen til showet.
Takk for at du var her i Aslo for en liten dag.
Ja, vi har bare rappet opp en klarene event på NewClear Energy her i D&B's headquarter i Aslo med deg,
som tar denne stedet. Så jeg har startet med å si, velkommen, jeg mener det var virkelig interessant,
og vi opptrykker deg at du tar tid til å joine oss, både her på stedet og i fokus, selvfølgelig.
Nukulær er gået til en utrolig, utrolig veldig tid nå, så det er virkelig en pleie,
og det er en tremendous masse globalt intres i teknologi.
Så jeg var glad i å spente en tid, og løp av en del av de kjøpene og nøyhverdene som tar til en industri nå.
Ja, ja, det intresse som ser til å være, jeg er ikke sikker om det er det korrekt,
men det intresse som ser definitivt til å være, å få opp her i Norge også, og vi kommer tilbake til det.
Men for å starte, for de som er du, eller de som er løsene som ikke vet deg,
hvordan starter du med en slags introduksjon av deg selv og din erfaring i dagig arbeid med nukulær energi?
Så jeg var interessant enough, jeg var en beskyttende soler energi-kvist for mange, mange år,
og jeg startet min karriere års gammel, faktisk i USA, som driver oljentangler rundt verden.
Og jeg finnes med det, og jeg har kvitt nok olje på å gå til åsjen, og jeg sa, at det er en annen solusjon.
Så jeg var meget interessert i soler energi og har spist en tid i Kyro og Somali,
og vi har adviset regjene på soler energi, og så var det en tid til å pumpere mer olje og soler energi-intresset
som vandt, og så har jeg kvitt på forskninger til å gå og investigere.
Og en av de ting som jeg endet å gjøre var å gå til den Jukka Moutains nukulær kvist i USA,
og vi hadde spennet på en time 6 billon dollar til å develope denne fasilitet, til å stå og spente nukulær kvist,
og vi var ikke gammel til å bruke den, så det har gammel til å være vant til å være vant til å kviste så mye
og ikke bruke den. Og en veldig måned jeg var opptatt av Michael Liebreik,
som var founder av NU Energy Finance, som var endelig kvist til Bloomberg,
som var en nukulær kvist i finans, og han fråte meg om jeg skulle være interessert i å
manage en forsknings-servis fokusering på nukulær energi, og det har vært en liten tid tid siden.
Så for de seneste siden, jeg har vært studerende i den nukulær energi-industrien,
jeg har visst nukulær reaktorer på hver enn enn en kontent, except for Antarctica,
hvor det ikke er. Og jeg er veldig fascineret med det promise nukulær energi
har til å få inn en siden av nukulær energi og siden av nukulær ønsker og
også få inn en siden av nukulær ekonomiske utstillinger som det verktes.
Ja. Du knows, Ejen Narkeld Evensson, min kollega som du også vet,
han er sånn den venner jeg går til når den kommer til nukulær her på D&B,
og han taler også meget om deg som en venner vi er lukkende til,
når det kommer til en balanser av nukulær. Så again, happy to have you here, Chris.
Og som du sa, jeg tror jeg visste mer enn en hand full av nukulær siden over en år
mellom dem, inkludering Fukushima. Så jeg tror jeg har lært det.
Ja, Fukushima var et veldig, veldig færdig event. Og hvis de jepene hadde
realized that such a severe seismic fault existed offshore Fukushima,
they probably would never have built the reactors over there. In fact, they sort of accentuated
the problem because Fukushima is built on a plateau and they excavated down in the plateau down
closer to sea level to reduce the head of the cooling water. So they made it more efficient,
but also by doing that they made it much more vulnerable to the tsunami which overtook
the facility. Very, very tragic development. You know, yesterday we had an internal teaching
with you here at D&B and then you start by asking us, have any of you ever been to a
nuclear facility? And your point by asking us this, I guess, was that if you have been
to a nuclear facility, you have a much, much better understanding of how complex it is.
Complex is probably an understatement. It really is a fascinating example of engineering achievement,
harnessing the power of the atom to generate electricity and to generate so much of it
in a carbon free method. It's really remarkable. I sort of firmly believe that without
advanced fission or fusion, possibly, you know, we're going to have a very, very difficult time
in global society to realize our net zero aspiration. So it's a fascinating piece of technology. It's a
very, very dense type of technology and it can produce a tremendous amount of electricity. And
we've had some unfortunate hiccups, but it is a very, very relevant technology going forward.
Ja. So from your global perspective, it's more nuclear energy a part of the solution we need to
succeed with the energy transition to make sure energy security is maintained, you know, that we
manage to meet the ever growing energy demand in the world. It's my hypothesis that net zero
without advanced fission or fusion is just hot air. We're just talking about it. It's going to play a
critical part, perhaps a small part, but a critical part in helping society achieve climate
objectives. I think it's absolutely essential. Just think about playing soccer or playing
basketball or football or so, and you have your best player and that best player is not
participating or contributing to the game and you're losing the game. And which is what I think
is happening with climate change and that we have technologies at our disposal, which we were really
not taking full advantage of it. And nuclear is one of those. It's an excellent technology with
which to combat climate change and achieve net zero. And there are several large institutions
that suggest the same that nuclear energy is becoming is a very, very critical tool for us to address
climate change and we should be employing more of it than we are. Yeah, so nuclear is definitely a
part of the energy mix solution sort of needed to take us where we want to go. I think it's
absolutely necessary, but the drivers for nuclear energy have sort of changed in the last several years.
We were the the the interest in nuclear energy recently has been its ability to deliver
tremendous amount of carbon free electricity to the grid from an environmental perspective. What
we're seeing now, which is more important in following the Ukraine Russia episode, is that
people realize the value of security, the value of having energy generated within the country.
And so countries and companies are looking at nuclear energy as a technology that can deliver
electricity that is homegrown to use an expression from from the US. And I think that's very,
very important that energy security is now becoming a very, very important component of
utility and hyperscaler investment strategies. Yeah, so in Norway, at least the way I see it,
the debate over nuclear power is somewhat polarized, or maybe deeply polarized,
is the right word. And it's also still on an early stage. So do you experience the same when
we're sitting other European countries? I think that polarization is a function of nuclear energy
as a matter of fact, in that it seems to be, you know, people are very much pro for it.
People are very much against it. The people who are against it are against it for
environmental reasons, for safety reasons, and many people say cost too much. But we've seen
examples where it is a cost effective solution. It is a technology that can be very, very safe,
and it plays a very, very important role for addressing some of the environmental
concerns. And so I think it's something that we need to embrace as society, those societies that can
provide carbon free energy, a lot of it to their citizens are going to be further ahead of the game
as far as developing and expanding their economies that those countries then do that, do that.
Many people on the renewable energy side advocate that it's a very, very relevant technology. Renewable energy
is a very good solution for part of the problem, but it's not a solution for the entire problem.
And I see the nuclear energy complementing the deployment of renewable energy because politically and
socially renewable energy has a higher ground. We're not going to displace that technology,
but we can use nuclear energy to complement renewable energy and to displace the fossil fuels that
are emitting so much carbon. So how important would you say the public perception of nuclear is to
get more projects started going forward? I think social acceptance is a very, very important issue.
We've seen places, perhaps the negative aspects of it, where you do not have a great social
acceptance of nuclear energy in Germany because of the Chernobyl accident and in Japan because of
the Fukushima accident. So there is a concerted effort in Japan to sort of rediscuss, address those
issues where people realize the value that nuclear energy can deliver. In Germany, they've
took some very, very bold stance and they've closed all of their nuclear power plants, 24 nuclear power
plants they've closed since Fukushima. And what's happened as a result, they're burning more fossil fuels,
emitting more carbon dioxide into the atmosphere. But it's a social issue and without having that
public acceptance and buy-in in Germany, it's going to be very difficult for the country to reverse
its decision. But we're seeing some people come to the terms that they need to go ahead and
reconsider, reevaluate. And there are other technologies, for example fusion technology,
which the Germans have a program into developing stellarator technology, which is a type of fusion
device that maybe can give them a pathway to using nuclear power without going down the advanced
fusion space. Yeah, yeah. When you visit different countries which have projects on their way or maybe they
have nuclear plants that are in operation, do you hear a lot of this sort of not in my backyard
argument? Oh, absolutely. The US has very ambitious goals outlined by the previous government,
the Biden administration, to add 200 gigawatts of nuclear power by 2050. That's a huge leap, a huge
challenge. And I've investigated some of the obstacles to going ahead and doing that. Biggest obstacle
perhaps will be the Trump administration, a change in energy policy. But aside from that, there is a lot of
nimbyism, not in my backyard. So you try to bring back a retired nuclear power plant, which is probably
the most least expensive and most cost effective and fastest way to bring back some nuclear assets.
That will, in the case of the Palisades nuclear power plant in Michigan, it will reduce CO2 emissions by
over 101 million tons in the lifetime of the extension. But people are opposed to that because
they are fearful of the nuclear power plant. They're fearful that they don't understand the dangers,
they don't understand the implication. To me, it's much easier to manage nuclear spent fuel
than to manage the CO2 that's going into the atmosphere from fossil fuel generation.
And unfortunately, that is what is going to be the case.
So can you try to give us an overview of where we are today in terms of global capacity and
how much projects that are planned and sort of also sort of what do you expect of
how much of the existing reactors are expected to be sort of taken out?
Ja, so we have about 440 nuclear power plants, plus or minus five or ten or so. I can't remember the
exact number, but something like 370 gigawatts of capacity. The COP28 announced plans for the lead
by the US and the UK to triple global capacity by 2050, so taking it to 1200 gigawatts of nuclear
capacity. So that's a very heavy lift for the nuclear industry for a variety of different reasons.
Is this possible?
I argue that certain countries will be able to triple their capacity and other countries will
struggle to do so. And there are several reasons why they will struggle. One is cost,
one is social and political acceptance and buy-in. And others would be
shortage of labor force, shortage of assets. So there's a variety of different things.
It's a very complex process. Politically, it's a very, very important decision that governments
will be making. Do we want to pursue nuclear power? And so on one side it's political
decision, and on the other side it's a technical and economic decision. And you have to have a good
technological approach, a good economic case, and a good political support. All three of those must be present
not to mention social support. You can't build nuclear power plants without support of the people
who are going to be local communities and who will be investing in that technology. So
it's doable if we come to realize that it's probably a very effective way for going
forward to address the two big issues, environmental and energy security issues.
So the planned capacity, it's not happening in the western world, is it? It's happening in China,
and it's happening in Russia, it's happening in all the countries.
Yeah, it's very disappointing as an energy analyst to see an imbalance globally whereas
two-thirds of the activity in nuclear power construction is going on in the Asia-Pacific region.
Europe and Middle East has about a third, and the Americas, North and South America,
have just a small fraction of that. And that's very, very disconcerting because it has to be a
global solution, and the US government is very much aware that they are behind the April,
and we have no nuclear projects under construction in America since sometime last year,
when we brought the last nuclear power project online. So it is imbalanced, the US government
and the Western Europeans are struggling to find a pathway to go forward and introduce more
nuclear power, but the costs are much higher in Western Europe and in America, sometimes five times
greater than some of the projects can be built for in China. And so one can understand very well why
the Chinese are building nuclear power plants because they can build them very effectively,
very quickly and economically, whereas the struggles that we've experienced in Western Europe
and in Europe suggest that these projects are going to be much more expensive,
and when you have a project that becomes more expensive, that invites opportunities from other
technologies to sort of take a share of that proposed capacity generation.
But I mean, if for example the Chinese manage to reduce construction time, to reduce costs,
shouldn't that be encouraging? Well, it certainly is, but given we have to understand that right now,
the Chinese are building 29 nuclear power plants. Nothing is being built in the US, nothing in Canada,
and in Western Europe, just a few old projects are still coming across the finish line.
And the Chinese are able to build for much less because they have a trained workforce,
they have tremendous government support, they have lower cost of labor,
they have lower interest rates, they have a less rigorous regulatory environment.
These are all things that the Europeans and the Americans need to sort of address and focus on,
so that we can build nuclear power plants at a reasonable cost, which past experience in the US
and in Europe suggests that we are struggling to do so.
So you had this really good explanation when it comes to the way forward.
You sort of said it like we have five pathways to nuclear.
Why don't you sort of try to explain that for us?
Because I think it will give us a good sort of overview of the different technologies,
the different way to sort of think of how we could increase capacity and so on.
Right, so if you're a utility executive, a politician, or a hyperscaler building data centers
and you need a tremendous amount of electricity, there are five pathways that nuclear can provide
electrons on the grid. The first pathway is large reactors, and there are several examples of that.
We've built the AP1000 in the United States, which is a Westinghouse reactor in the UK and in France
and in Finland. We've been the European Pressurized React, the EPR. The Koreans are building their technology.
They've done it very, very successfully in the United Arab Emirates, and they've received the contract
from the Czech government to possibly build two more over there.
Så de lille reaktoren er teknologiske som har vært for lang tid.
Det er veldig lille tekniske risk.
Det har vært til 2015, 2016.
Absolutt. Det er veldig lille tekniske risk, men det er et konstruks- og operatøy risk.
Så å få dem tilbake på veldig tid og på budget har vært et problem for
de vinterne.
Det neste pathet er små modulereaktoren, og dette er en innovasjon, hvor du har en typisk
lille reaktor som er 1 gigawatt eller 1000 megawatt. En små modulereaktor er typisk 300 megawatt,
og til å overrøde røysten for teknologiske åpner, det er 470 megawatt.
Den principale innovasjon er at du tar en lille reaktor og skrinker den, og med å gjøre det,
du skrinker oppfremdighet og skrinker tid til market som tar til å bygge det,
som skrinker din intreskjøp. Det er en veldig interessant, og det er en masse entusiasmer
for små reaktoren i verden. Det har en lille risker på et pathet.
Hvis der er en annen små reaktorer som er i veld,
advanced reaktorer som er i China og i Russia, de landene er gammel mer.
Nå er små reaktorer i verden.
Det næste teknologiske mål er det jeg kaller advanced reaktorer. Det er en lille
diskussi med små modulereaktorer og advanced reaktorer som skal være røyst sammen,
men jeg har et stort stort advanced reaktorer fra små reaktorer som er i veld,
fordi du ser på en annen teknologiske mål, for eksempel en fast reaktor,
eller en høy temperatør gas reaktorer eller en mål som er små reaktorer.
De er teknologiske mål som kan provide en søker operasjon, kan provide en lille
spennende operasjon, kan adreses til de langt spennende fulgninger, kan adreses til
pluferasjoner. De er alle vært prosutt i en veldig av forskjellige
sammen med kunder og landene i verden, og de representerer et teknologisk
forkring og innovasjon som går beyond just shrinkage av en lille reaktor, og de kan
være bedre for å pair med internett og renuabeller med å forkommende
on-site storage på det. De 4te passfægene er veldig små nukle
større planer som vi kaller mikroreaktorer. De er allige speciellt mindre
som 50 megawatt, og det er en masse interesser i dem for å supply
remote mining areas og norske latitøs og også supply data centers og også for
militære baser fordi de er med små nukle større planer. Det treffer seg til
å protecte denne eventen så du kan locere dem på en militære bas.
Du har en eksting sekuritetsparameter så du kan løse kvar som
er nøsselig for det. Og finally, den seneste passfægene, som er veldig
veldig skjære, er å svare på å funge. Og fungen har vært
karakterisert som teknologi som er veldig svært å realise.
Det er det processen som forstår siden. Derefter at det er splittende
atomer med fysjon, er vi kombinerende som liten atomer med fungen og
det sker mer energi. Og det er tre passfægene til å
skjære fungen. Det er internasjonale klæberation, etter
fysjelder i south av fransk. Mange landene har næsjel program.
The U.S., U.K., Korea, Germany, Japan, og China all har lokale
næsjel fungen program. Og det mest skjære er det
private kommersjonale investering i fungenkompagene.
De er de som er spesifikt til å tage fysjonne
å kreere kvarplanser til å produere karpenefri
nøglerne på grønnen. Vi håper å se det skjære
i den første 2030-er. Så far har de vært
sammen med 7,5 og 8 billon kroner av private investeringen
i alle de ledende fylanserpillene i verden.
Jeff Bezos, Sam Altman, Bragel through energy,
som er en kongromerasjon, en veldig fylanserpill,
de er alle kjøkke på teknologi. Det er veldig
promissing, men det er jo ikke forventet. Vi
har entenvist en signifisk brevkjøk som har
vært i de næste sierne år som vil sige at det er en
veldig teknologi, og så er problemet
skjøkket fra en sidenisk problem til en
engeneering problem som skal være løsere å se og
delivered. Den første company som er i ledet
presentligere nå, Commonwealth Fusion, har
already picked their first site in Virginia to supply
data centers, og den første site er
ganske å være søkt som en time i den nye
fyr i the UK. Så SMR, det har vært en
sørgjende bussverd, det ser ut som om en time.
Så av alle de fem pathways er SMR det
fastest og kanskje det mest realistisk
måte for, eller er det ikke det?

risk profile, kvoss profile, og

den nye termet, hvis du skal være delivered

representerer en løsere riske og
den kjøkeste teknologi for å kunne
gjøre så. Ja, jeg tror du har sagt
som for komparisering, de løsere reaktorer,
det er en sort av dinasor.
De løsere reaktorer er en åld teknologi,

Og så er det mer svært og mer
